annotator_1.text: Cross-domain transfer in RL has two fundamental and intertwined challenges: (i) The source and target domains can have distinct state space or action space, and this makes direct transfer infeasible and thereby requires more sophisticated inter-domain mappings; (ii) The transferability of a source-domain model in RL is not easily identifiable a priori, and hence CDRL can be prone to negative effect during transfer.
annotator_1.source_quote: Despite its potential, cross-domain transfer in RL is known to have two fundamental and intertwined challenges: (i) The source and target domains can have distinct state space or action space, and this makes direct transfer infeasible and thereby requires more sophisticated inter-domain mappings; (ii) The transferability of a source-domain model in RL is not easily identifiable a priori, and hence CDRL can be prone to negative effect during transfer.

annotator_1.text: Q-Avatar combines the Q functions from both the source and target domains with an adaptive hyperparameter-free weight function.
annotator_1.source_quote: Then, we propose $Q$Avatar, which combines the Q functions from both the source and target domains with an adaptive hyperparameter-free weight function.

annotator_1.text: Q-Avatar achieves reliable transfer in the sense that it effectively leverages a source-domain Q function for knowledge transfer to the target domain.
annotator_1.source_quote: Through this design, we characterize the convergence behavior of $Q$Avatar and show that $Q$Avatar achieves reliable transfer in the sense that it effectively leverages a source-domain Q function for knowledge transfer to the target domain.

annotator_1.text: Q-Avatar achieves favorable transferability across various RL benchmark tasks, including locomotion and robot arm manipulation.
annotator_1.source_quote: Through experiments, we demonstrate that $Q$Avatar achieves favorable transferability across various RL benchmark tasks, including locomotion and robot arm manipulation.

annotator_2.text: Cross-domain reinforcement learning (CDRL) improves the data efficiency of RL by leveraging data samples from a source domain to facilitate learning in a similar target domain.
annotator_2.source_quote: Cross-domain reinforcement learning (CDRL) is meant to improve the data efficiency of RL by leveraging the data samples collected from a source domain to facilitate the learning in a similar target domain.

annotator_2.text: Cross-domain transfer in RL has two fundamental and intertwined challenges.
annotator_2.source_quote: Despite its potential, cross-domain transfer in RL is known to have two fundamental and intertwined challenges

annotator_2.text: Source and target domains can have distinct state space or action space.
annotator_2.source_quote: The source and target domains can have distinct state space or action space

annotator_2.text: Distinct state or action spaces between domains make direct transfer infeasible.
annotator_2.source_quote: The source and target domains can have distinct state space or action space, and this makes direct transfer infeasible

annotator_2.text: Distinct state or action spaces between domains require more sophisticated inter-domain mappings.
annotator_2.source_quote: The source and target domains can have distinct state space or action space, and this makes direct transfer infeasible and thereby requires more sophisticated inter-domain mappings

annotator_2.text: The transferability of a source-domain model in RL is not easily identifiable a priori.
annotator_2.source_quote: The transferability of a source-domain model in RL is not easily identifiable a priori

annotator_2.text: CDRL can be prone to negative effect during transfer.
annotator_2.source_quote: The transferability of a source-domain model in RL is not easily identifiable a priori, and hence CDRL can be prone to negative effect during transfer

annotator_2.text: The paper proposes to tackle the two CDRL challenges through cross-domain Bellman consistency and hybrid critic.
annotator_2.source_quote: In this paper, we propose to jointly tackle these two challenges through the lens of cross-domain Bellman consistency and hybrid critic.

annotator_2.text: Cross-domain Bellman consistency is introduced as a way to measure transferability of a source-domain model.
annotator_2.source_quote: Specifically, we first introduce the notion of cross-domain Bellman consistency as a way to measure transferability of a source-domain model.

annotator_2.text: Q-Avatar combines Q functions from both source and target domains.
annotator_2.source_quote: Then, we propose $Q$Avatar, which combines the Q functions from both the source and target domains

annotator_2.text: Q-Avatar uses an adaptive hyperparameter-free weight function.
annotator_2.source_quote: Then, we propose $Q$Avatar, which combines the Q functions from both the source and target domains with an adaptive hyperparameter-free weight function.

annotator_2.text: The convergence behavior of Q-Avatar is characterized in the paper.
annotator_2.source_quote: Through this design, we characterize the convergence behavior of $Q$Avatar
Through this design, we characterize the convergence behavior of $Q$Avatar and show that $Q$Avatar achieves reliable transfer

annotator_2.text: Q-Avatar effectively leverages a source-domain Q function for knowledge transfer to the target domain.
annotator_2.source_quote: Through this design, we characterize the convergence behavior of $Q$Avatar and show that $Q$Avatar achieves reliable transfer in the sense that it effectively leverages a source-domain Q function for knowledge transfer to the target domain.

annotator_2.text: Q-Avatar achieves favorable transferability across various RL benchmark tasks.
annotator_2.source_quote: Through experiments, we demonstrate that $Q$Avatar achieves favorable transferability across various RL benchmark tasks

annotator_2.text: The RL benchmark tasks tested include locomotion and robot arm manipulation.
annotator_2.source_quote: Through experiments, we demonstrate that $Q$Avatar achieves favorable transferability across various RL benchmark tasks, including locomotion and robot arm manipulation.

annotator_2.text: The code for Q-Avatar is available at https://rl-bandits-lab.github.io/Cross-Domain-RL/.
